title: The Body's Wisdom: Learning to Listen After Years of Override
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: Trauma teaches us to mistrust our bodies. Learn how to listen to your body's wisdom, understand the language of your nervous system, and reclaim your inner authority.
imageDescription: A soft, golden light illuminates a delicate, mended ceramic bowl, its cracks filled with shimmering gold. The bowl rests on a warm, wooden surface, suggesting gentle repair and the beauty of a healed history. The scene is quiet, luminous, and deeply peaceful.
htmlContent:
Nobody tells you that the first casualty of trauma is not your innocence, but your trust in your own body. We are taught, through events or through atmospheres, that the signals our bodies send - the tightening in the chest, the sudden urge to flee, the inexplicable wave of exhaustion... are not reliable data but hysterical noise to be managed, suppressed, and ultimately, overridden.

The Anatomy of Override

The architecture of this internal betrayal is built slowly, brick by brick, in the name of survival. For a child navigating an environment where their authentic responses are met with punishment, dismissal, or further threat, disconnection becomes a brilliant strategy. The body’s wisdom, which speaks in the pre-verbal language of sensation and impulse, is deemed a liability. So, the mind, in its incredible capacity for adaptation, learns to build a fortress around the heart and a gag order for the gut. It learns to dissociate from the raw data of experience and live instead in stories... narratives that make the unbearable bearable, often by casting the self as the problem to be fixed.

The work of healing, then, is not about learning something new but about remembering something ancient: the body’s native intelligence. It is a process of archeology, of gently brushing away the layers of conditioning and narrative to uncover the truth that has been waiting patiently beneath the surface all along. It is the slow, courageous process of returning to the body, not as a problem to be solved, but as a home to be reclaimed.

The Unconscious Sentry: Your Nervous System’s Hidden Logic

To understand why listening to the body feels so foreign and often so frightening, we must look beneath the level of our conscious thoughts to the silent, powerful processes of the nervous system. This is the area of what researcher **Stephen Porges** calls “neuroception.” Unlike perception, which is a cognitive act, neuroception is the nervous system’s instantaneous, non-conscious scanning of the environment for cues of safety, danger, and life threat. For a deeper look at the mechanics, one might explore the fundamentals of what polyvagal theory is and how it maps our responses.

What emerges is why appealing to logic so often fails. You cannot reason with neuroception. You cannot talk your nervous system out of a response that it has already initiated based on its own deeply ingrained, sensory-based logic. The felt sense of danger will always trump the intellectual knowledge of safety. The body’s testimony, written in the language of adrenaline and cortisol, of muscle tension and shallow breath, is simply more compelling to the primal parts of the brain than any story the neocortex can offer. The path forward does not lie in arguing with this internal sentry, but in learning to understand its language and, over time, providing it with new experiences that can begin to recalibrate its assessments.

Learning the Grammar of Sensation

If we are to move from a state of internal override to one of internal listening, we must first become students of a language we were taught to forget: the grammar of sensation. The body does not speak in well-reasoned arguments or linear narratives; it communicates through the subtle and not-so-subtle shifts in physical experience. It speaks in the flutter of the stomach, the heat rising in the face, the deep ache in the shoulders, the sudden wave of fatigue. They are the body’s way of saying, “Pay attention. Something is happening.” For those of us who have spent a lifetime living from the neck up, this can feel like learning a foreign tongue with no dictionary.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link).

The breath is often the most accessible entry point into this practice. It is a constant, rhythmic anchor to the present moment, a physiological process that is happening right now, regardless of the stories the mind is spinning. Simply noticing the fact of the breath - the air entering, the chest rising and falling - can be a real act of returning. It is about offering it our quiet, non-judgmental attention. It is about discovering the difference between thinking about the body and actually inhabiting it, a distinction that is central to understanding interoception, the sixth sense of our internal state.

Sit with that.

When the Body's Testimony Contradicts the Mind's Story

One of the most disorienting and yet important moments in the road of healing is when we begin to notice the striking chasm between what the mind insists is true and what the body is actually experiencing. We might be at a dinner party, surrounded by friends, and the mind says, “That becomes fun. I am safe. The shoulders are creeping up toward the ears, the jaw is clenched, and there is a powerful, inexplicable urge to disappear. Or we might be in a relationship that, on paper, is perfect, yet the body exists in a state of low-grade dread, a subtle but persistent feeling of being trapped. This cognitive dissonance is the hallmark of a nervous system still operating from the script of the past.

The turning point arrives when the real work of somatic inquiry begins, a process detailed by pioneers in modalities available through resources like Somatic Experiencing International. It is the practice of holding both realities ~ the mind’s story and the body’s testimony - with a spacious and curious awareness, without needing to immediately resolve the contradiction. It is about understanding that the body’s responses, especially those that seem irrational, are often unconscious echoes of emotional flashbacks, pointing us toward the parts of our history that still need to be met with presence and compassion.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link).

The Slow Practice of Re-Inhabitation

Re-establishing a connection with the body after years, or even decades, of override is not a dramatic event but a slow, incremental practice of re-inhabitation. It is a process built of a thousand tiny moments of choosing to turn toward sensation instead of away from it. The goal here is not to immediately “fix” or “calm” the body’s signals of distress. The initial practice is much simpler and far more deep: it is the practice of listening. It is the willingness to be with what is true in our sensory experience, without judgment and without a demand that it be different.

The Architecture of Embodied Wisdom

As the practice of listening deepens and the capacity to stay present with sensation grows, something striking begins to shift. We move from a life directed by the rigid, top-down control of the cognitive mind to a life that is informed by the fluid, bottom-up intelligence of the body. It turns out to be the emergence of embodied wisdom. It is not a mystical state but a deeply practical and grounded way of being in the world. It is the difference between knowing the map and knowing the territory. Decision-making begins to change. Instead of relying solely on pro-and-con lists, we start to factor in the body’s vote. A job offer might look perfect on paper, but if the body responds with a subtle but persistent sense of contraction, we learn to take that data seriously.

This road is, in many ways, a quiet revolution. It is the reclaiming of a fundamental human birthright: the right to inhabit our own skin, to trust our own experience, and to be guided by the wisdom that is encoded in our very cells. It is a move away from the endless and exhausting project of self-improvement and toward the much deeper, more satisfying work of self-understanding. We stop trying to fix ourselves and instead learn to listen to ourselves. In this listening, we discover a source of intelligence, resilience, and guidance that has been there all along, waiting patiently for us to come home. It is a truth that cannot be fully grasped by the intellect, because it is a lived reality.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the difference between this and just "listening to your gut"?

While similar to 'listening to your gut,' this approach is more specific and grounded in neuroscience. It involves tracking subtle sensations throughout the body and understanding their link to your trauma history and nervous system state, going beyond a simple 'good' or 'bad' feeling.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

My body just feels numb. How can I listen to something I can't feel?

Numbness is not an absence of feeling, but a feeling itself ~ an intelligent survival response of shutdown. Don't force feeling; bring gentle, curious attention to the numbness. Where is it? What is its texture? Allowing it to be there without judgment is often what allows underlying sensations to slowly and safely emerge.

Here is the thing, though.

Is it possible to listen too much to the body and become anxious?

Yes, one can become hyper-focused on sensations, leading to anxiety or 'somatic hypervigilance.' The key is the quality of attention. Anxious scanning is fear-driven, searching for what's wrong. Embodied listening is rooted in curious acceptance, gently noticing what is present without a need to fix it, teaching the nervous system that sensation isn't a threat.

How does this relate to memory and emotional flashbacks?

The body is the primary vehicle for trauma memory, stored as sensory fragments, not narrative. An emotional flashback is the re-experiencing of traumatic feelings without the visual memory. Listening to the body is key to identifying flashbacks and grounding in the present, allowing the intense energy to move through you without getting lost in the old story.

A body tool that some find helpful for releasing chronic tension is Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set (paid link).

The path back to the body is a spiral, returning to the same themes with deepening awareness. It is the slow work of learning to trust that you are your own wisest counsel, that the truth you seek is written in the architecture of your being. It is the tender act of returning, again and again, to the quiet wisdom waiting for you.

Are you ready to stop preparing and start practicing?
